cards.text: What are the three trimesters of pregnancy and what key events occur in each?
cards.answer: First trimester (weeks 1-12): organogenesis, most vulnerable to teratogens. Second trimester (weeks 13-24): rapid growth, organ maturation. Third trimester (weeks 25-birth): organ maturation, weight gain, lung surfactant/1st organogenesis, 2nd growth, 3rd maturation

cards.text: What is the difference between an embryo and a foetus?
cards.answer: Embryo: weeks 1-8 (period of organogenesis, all major organs form). Foetus: week 9 to birth (period of growth and maturation)/embryo is weeks 1-8; foetus is week 9 to birth

tip.tip: The embryonic period is the critical period for malformations

cards.text: What does viability mean and at what gestational age is it typically reached?
cards.answer: Viability is the ability of the foetus to survive outside the uterus; typically reached at 24 weeks with intensive neonatal care/ability to survive outside uterus at approximately 24 weeks

tip.tip: Lung surfactant production is the key limiting factor

cards.text: What is a teratogen? Name three examples.
cards.answer: An agent that causes structural or functional abnormalities in the developing embryo. Examples: thalidomide (limb defects), alcohol (fetal alcohol syndrome), rubella virus (congenital rubella)/agent causing birth defects

tip.tip: The effect depends on the timing, dose, and genetic susceptibility

cards.text: What is the critical period of development?
cards.answer: The time window during which a developing organ is most susceptible to teratogenic insult; coincides with the period of active morphogenesis/window of maximum susceptibility during organ formation

tip.tip: Heart: weeks 3-6; limbs: weeks 4-7; brain: weeks 3-16

cards.text: What are the subdivisions of embryology?
cards.answer: General embryology (gametogenesis, fertilisation, gastrulation), systemic embryology (organ system development), comparative embryology (across species), experimental embryology (mechanisms)/general systemic comparative experimental

tip.tip: General embryology is covered first in the NMC curriculum

cards.text: What is the all-or-none period in embryology?
cards.answer: The first two weeks after fertilisation, when teratogen exposure either kills the embryo (miscarriage) or has no effect because totipotent cells compensate/first 2 weeks: either lethal or no effect

tip.tip: Totipotent cells can replace damaged cells during this period

cards.text: What percentage of live births in India have congenital anomalies?
cards.answer: Approximately 3% of all live births/3 percent

tip.tip: Second leading cause of infant mortality in India
